item.text: {"summary":"Live search suggests a remaining distinct first-pass feature direction: target-free empirical CDF, tail-risk, and rank-normal preprocessing features that expose rare photometric/redshift regimes without adding supervised meta-models or another domain-specific rule set.","hypotheses":[{"title":"Empirical CDF Tail Copula Features","summary":"Represent each object by pooled and metadata-conditional percentile, tail-surprise, and rank-normal features to expose rare SDSS-like regimes.","feature_family":"ecdf_tail_copula_preprocessing","feature_strategy":"Start from raw alpha, delta, u, g, r, i, z, redshift, spectral_type, galaxy_population, plus a compact set of adjacent and broad color indices. For each continuous anchor feature, fit empirical CDF transforms using fold-train plus test covariates only, then add pooled percentile rank, clipped logit-rank, Gaussian normal-score rank, lower-tail rank, upper-tail rank, two-sided tail probability, and -log(tail_probability). Repeat the same ECDF features within spectral_type and galaxy_population groups when group size is sufficient, falling back to pooled values for small groups. Add row summaries such as max/mean/sum tail surprise, number of features in 1%, 2.5%, and 5% tails, counts of blue-band versus red-band tail events, and a simple covariance or Euclidean distance in rank-normal color-redshift space. Winsorize only the raw continuous inputs at extreme pooled quantiles before model fitting, while keeping the tail indicators as explicit features. Do not use class labels, auxiliary SDSS labels, nearest-neighbor class scores, id, or intermediate class-prediction models.","baseline_model_panel":"Use a small reusable balanced multiclass panel: a standardized linear classifier for dense transformed features, a shallow/randomized tree baseline, and one or two simple gradient-boosted tree models with class balancing and native or one-hot categorical handling, such as CatBoost with auto_class_weights=\"Balanced\" and LightGBM with class_weight=\"balanced\" if available.","model_panel_rationale":"The linear model tests whether rank-normalized margins create separable additive signal, while tree and boosted-tree models test whether tail-count and group-conditional rarity interactions help without making the hypothesis depend on model tuning.","validation_strategy":"Use 5-fold StratifiedKFold scored by balanced accuracy. For each fold, fit ECDF, group-ECDF, winsorization, and rank-normal maps on fold-train plus test covariates, then transform fold-validation; for final test predictions, refit those target-free maps on full train plus test covariates.","materialization_hint":"Implement ECDF features as reusable transformers over a fixed numeric feature list. Clip percentile ranks away from exactly 0 and 1 before logit or inverse-normal transforms. Log per-feature tail rates and group fallback counts so the run can detect no-op or unstable group ECDF behavior.","expected_signal":"If useful, CV should show small but consistent balanced-accuracy gains, especially for minority STAR or QSO recall, and boosted trees should rank tail-surprise summaries, group-conditional tail features, or rank-normal redshift/color distances above many raw magnitude inputs.","novelty_confidence":"medium","risk":"This may partly overlap with prior rank/frequency feature blocks if those already included pooled percentile ranks, but the novelty is the explicit ECDF tail-surprise, group-conditional tail, and rank-normal copula representation. It can be a no-op if the synthetic train/test distributions are already smooth, and transductive CDF fitting must remain strictly target-free to avoid validation optimism.","sources":["https://arxiv.org/abs/2201.00382","https://arxiv.org/abs/2309.10194","https://arxiv.org/abs/2112.10078","https://arxiv.org/abs/1410.4696"]}]}
